


# Introduction
New York City is one of the largest energy consumers in the United States, with its building stock accounting for approximately 70% of the city's total greenhouse gas emissions. Understanding what drives energy consumption in buildings is critical for informing policy decisions, guiding retrofits, and achieving the city's climate goals under Local Law 97, which mandates significant emissions reductions from large buildings by 2030.

The prediction research question for this project is: Can we predict a building's Site Energy Use Intensity (kBtu/ft²) based on its physical and operational characteristics? This project aims to predict a building's Site Energy Use Intensity (Site EUI, measured in kBtu/ft²), which represents the amount of energy a building consumes per square foot per year. Site EUI is a widely used benchmarking metric because it normalizes energy consumption by building size, allowing fair comparisons across buildings of different scales. A lower Site EUI indicates a more energy-efficient building. Since Site EUI is a continuous numeric variable, this is a regression problem.

Accurately predicting Site EUI based on a building's physical and operational characteristics, such as size, age, and occupancy type, is highly valuable for multiple stakeholders. Building owners and property managers can use such a model to identify cost-saving opportunities and prioritize energy retrofits. Policymakers can use findings about which features are most predictive of high energy use to guide future building codes and enforcement strategies. More broadly, this project contributes to ongoing research on urban energy consumption and climate accountability.
 

# Data Source
The data used in this project comes from NYC Open Data's Energy and Water Data Disclosure for Local Law 84 (2021), which contains energy benchmarking reports submitted annually by owners of large NYC buildings. The dataset contains over 25,000 observations and includes variables describing building characteristics, location, occupancy, and energy consumption metrics.

NYC Open Data. (2021). Energy and Water Data Disclosure for Local Law 84 2021 (Data for Calendar Year 2020). City of New York. https://data.cityofnewyork.us/Environment/Energy-and-Water-Data-Disclosure-for-Local-Law-84-/usc3-8zwd/about_data

# Data Overview
Data Quality & Missingness
The raw dataset contains 28,067 observations and 250 variables, of which 19 are numeric and 231 are categorical. A missingness inspection revealed that several variables have notable proportions of missing values. For numeric variables, missing values will be imputed using the median to avoid sensitivity to outliers. For categorical variables, rows with missing values will be removed. Given the large number of observations, this approach is unlikely to meaningfully skew model estimates.

Target Variable: Site EUI
The response variable, Site EUI (kBtu/ft²), was heavily right-skewed in its raw form, with a small number of buildings reporting extremely high energy use intensity — likely large or industrial facilities with outsized consumption. To address this, a log transformation (log1p) was applied, resulting in a more symmetric, approximately normal distribution centered around 90 kBtu/ft². This transformation stabilizes variance and improves model performance for algorithms sensitive to distributional assumptions.
Outliers on the higher end of the distribution are expected given the nature of the data and will be accounted for during feature engineering and model building.




# Methods
Data Splitting
The dataset was split into training and testing sets using an 80/20 split, with 80% of observations allocated to training and 20% reserved for final model evaluation. Stratified sampling on the target variable site_eui was used to ensure both sets had a similar distribution of energy use intensity values. This is a regression prediction problem, as the goal is to predict a continuous numeric outcome.

Resampling
To evaluate model performance during tuning and selection, 5-fold cross-validation with 4 repeats was applied to the training set, again stratified by site_eui. This resulted in 20 total resamples, providing a robust and stable estimate of out-of-sample performance while reducing the risk of overfitting to any single fold.

# Model Types
7 model types were fit and compared:

Null Model — a baseline that predicts the mean of site_eui for all observations, serving as a performance floor
Baseline Model — a simple reference model used to contextualize the performance of more complex models
Linear Regression (LM) — a parametric model that assumes a linear relationship between predictors and the outcome
Elastic Net (EN) — a regularized regression model combining L1 and L2 penalties, useful for handling correlated predictors and performing variable selection
K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) — a non-parametric model that predicts based on the average outcome of the most similar observations in the training set
Random Forest (RF) — an ensemble of decision trees using bagging, which captures non-linear relationships and interaction effects
Boosted Tree (BT/XGBoost) — a sequential ensemble method that iteratively fits trees to residuals, offering strong predictive performance on structured tabular data. 

# Tuning Parameters
The boosted tree model required hyperparameter tuning. The following parameters were tuned using a regular grid search:

trees — number of boosting iterations (searched over 200–1000)
tree_depth — maximum depth of each tree (searched over 3–8)
learn_rate — step size shrinkage to prevent overfitting (searched on log scale over 10⁻³–10⁻¹)
min_n — minimum number of observations required to split a node (searched over 5–30)

A grid of 3 levels per parameter was used, resulting in 81 candidate combinations evaluated across all resamples.
Recipes
A standard preprocessing recipe was applied to all models, including imputation of missing values, dummy encoding of nominal predictors, and normalization of all numeric predictors to have mean zero and standard deviation one. Normalization was applied primarily to benefit the linear regression model, as tree-based methods are scale-invariant.

# Model Selection Metric
To compare models and select a final one, Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) was used as the primary evaluation metric. RMSE measures the average magnitude of prediction errors in the same units as the outcome variable site_eui, making it straightforward to interpret. Lower RMSE means that the model's predictions are closer to the actual energy use values on average. RMSE was preferred over Mean Absolute Error (MAE) because it squares the errors before averaging, which means larger mistakes are penalized more heavily. This felt appropriate for this problem since significantly over- or under-predicting a building's energy use would be more problematic than small, consistent errors. The model and hyperparameter combination that achieved the lowest average RMSE across all cross-validation resamples was ultimately chosen as the final model.


Comment on Generative AI Use
I used Generative AI to help organize my ideas and to explain my reasoning for my metrics as well as for tuning parameters. I used AI to help me analyze the autoplots for each model so that the parameters used for each model were as accurate as possible. In addition, I used it for the explanation for each model. 

Appendix: Tuning Parameter Analysis
Tuning Parameters Analysis of Autoplots

EN plots
autoen1 shows RMSE improving (dropping from ~76 to ~70) as regularization increases, then flattening out, with the lasso proportion having little effect. autoen2 shows the opposite pattern, starting at a better RMSE of ~66 with low regularization and worsening as regularization increases, making the 2nd recipe the overall better-performing recipe out of the two.

KNN plots
autoknn1 shows a clear U-shape with RMSE bottoming out at ~62.1 around k=13–15 neighbors before rising steadily to ~62.6, making that the optimal k. autoknn2 shows a steep decline from ~67 at k=1 that flattens into a broad minimum of ~64.1 around k=30–40, with a much wider range of acceptable k values but an overall worse best RMSE compared to r1.




Appendix EDA:
Distribution of Site EUI
Plots(site_eui_histogram_log & site_eui_histogram)
Transforming to log scale reveals the underlying shape much more clearly: the distribution becomes approximately normal, centered around 80–120 kBtu/ft², with a gentle taper on both sides. Better for linear models. 

Top Predictors
Electricity Use (Grid Purchase) shows a weak positive relationship with a flattening loess curve, huge variance, suggesting electricity explains some but not all of the EUI story.

Natural Gas Use has a striking inverted-U loess curve, peaking around 1.5×10⁹ kBtu before declining. Buildings with extreme gas use may be large industrial or specialty properties where floor area dominates the denominator of EUI. This variable may need to be removed. 

Hospital Floor Area and Laboratory Floor Area both show the bulk of data jammed at zero with a few extreme outliers.

Occupancy shows discrete clustering suggesting it may be ordinal or bucketed rather than continuous. The flat loess confirms weak direct linear correlation with EUI.

Supermarket/Grocery Floor Area mirrors the hospital/lab pattern highly sparse with most buildings at zero.

Overall, the extreme outlier points visible across all  panels suggest that outlier treatment and log transformation of predictors will be critical before fitting any model.

Site Eui by Borough
The median EUI is remarkably consistent across all five boroughs, all sitting around 50–150 kBtu/ft². However, Manhattan stands out with the single most extreme outlier (~6,800 kBtu/ft²), which likely corresponds to a hospital, data center, or lab. Queens also shows a notable high outlier (~1,200). Staten Island has fewer extreme values and a higher median. Borough alone is probably not a strong standalone predictor. 

Median Site EUI by Property
The spread across property types has a large range, confirming that property type should be a primary feature in any predictive model.
